From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [N$](/w/index.php?title=N$&redirect=no "N$"))

Currency of Namibia

For earlier Namibian currencies, see [South West African mark](/wiki/South_West_African_mark "South West African mark") and [South West African pound](/wiki/South_West_African_pound "South West African pound").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Namibia-Money01.jpg/330px-Namibia-Money01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Namibia-Money01.jpg)

Old N$10 banknote and coins

The **Namibia dollar** ([symbol](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol"): $ or N$; [code](/wiki/ISO_4217 "ISO 4217"): **NAD**) has been the [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency") of [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") since 1993. It is normally abbreviated with the [dollar sign](/wiki/Dollar_sign "Dollar sign") (**$**), or alternatively **N$** to distinguish it from other currencies called “[dollar](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar").” It is divided into 100 [cents](/wiki/Cent_(currency) "Cent (currency)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibian_dollar&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The dollar replaced the [South African rand](/wiki/South_African_rand "South African rand"), which had been the country's currency while it was under [South African](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") rule as [South-West Africa](/wiki/South-West_Africa "South-West Africa") from 1920 until 1990, at par. The rand is still [legal tender](/wiki/Legal_tender "Legal tender"), as the Namibian dollar is [linked](/wiki/Fixed_exchange_rate_system "Fixed exchange rate system") to the South African rand and can be exchanged on a one-to-one basis locally. Namibia was also part of the [Common Monetary Area](/wiki/Common_Monetary_Area "Common Monetary Area") from independence in 1990 until the introduction of the dollar in 1993.

In the beginning, alternative names for the Namibian dollar were suggested, including *Namibian kalahar*, referencing the Kalahari Desert in the east of Namibia, but ultimately the government settled on the name *Namibian dollar*. The first notes were issued on 15 September 1993.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The [Bank of Namibia](/wiki/Bank_of_Namibia "Bank of Namibia") issued the first banknotes on 15 September 1993 and, in December, issued the first national coins.

## Coins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibian_dollar&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Coins")]

[Coins](/wiki/Coin "Coin") in circulation

| denomination: | obverse: | reverse: |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 5c |  |  |
| 10c |  |  |
| 50c |  |  |
| N$1 |  |  |
| N$5 |  |  |

Years of mintage are 1993, 1996, 1998, 2000, 2002, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2012, 2015. The cent coins are made of [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel") [plated](/wiki/Plated "Plated") [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") and the dollar coins of [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass"). From January 2019, 5c coins are no longer produced, but they are still used for daily transactions.

## Banknotes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibian_dollar&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Banknotes")]

The following [banknotes](/wiki/Banknote "Banknote") are currently in circulation:

* N$10
* N$20
* N$30 (commemorative)[[2]](#cite_note-30nad-2)
* N$50
* N$60 (commemorative)[[3]](#cite_note-60nad-3)
* N$100
* N$200

Historically, [Kaptein Hendrik Witbooi](/wiki/Hendrik_Witbooi_(Nama_chief) "Hendrik Witbooi (Nama chief)"), once chief of the [Nama people](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people") and instrumental in leading the revolts against German rule at the turn of the 20th century, has been depicted on all Namibian banknotes. However, on 21 March 2012, the [Bank of Namibia](/wiki/Bank_of_Namibia "Bank of Namibia") introduced a new series of banknotes to be issued in May 2012. The new family of banknotes will have the same denomination structure as the current series. All denominations have improved anti-counterfeiting features, and the portrait of Kaptein Hendrik Witbooi is retained for all but the 10 and 20 dollar notes, which feature a new portrait of [Sam Nujoma](/wiki/Sam_Nujoma "Sam Nujoma"), the founding president and father of the Namibian nation.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The Bank of Namibia has discovered that the diamond-shaped optically variable ink patch on the N$10 and N$20 notes was cracking after multiple folding and handling. The Bank of Namibia has recently issued in limited quantity, the N$10 and N$20 notes on paper with improved quality and shifted the placement of the diamond-shaped optically variable ink feature.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On 21 March 2020, the Bank of Namibia unveiled a new 30 dollar polymer note to commemorate the 30th anniversary of independence. The theme of the note is '3 Decades of Peace and Stability', represented by the smooth transition of power between the three presidents since independence.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Test coins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibian_dollar&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Test coins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/M%C3%BCnze-Namibia-10_Mark-1990-Probe-verso.png/250px-M%C3%BCnze-Namibia-10_Mark-1990-Probe-verso.png)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnze-Namibia-10_Mark-1990-Probe-verso.png)

10 marks essai (reverse)

During the planning phase of the introduction of a new national currency replacing the South African rand, the newly founded [Bank of Namibia](/wiki/Bank_of_Namibia "Bank of Namibia") minted a proof series of coins denominated in [dollars](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar") as well as in [marks](/wiki/Mark_(money) "Mark (money)"), for the consideration of the Namibian [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Finance_minister "Finance minister"). The decision then fell in favour of the name ‘dollar’ for the new currency.

The proof series consisted of four different [coins](/wiki/Coin "Coin"): 1 mark, 1 dollar (both in [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper")/[nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel")), 10 marks and 10 dollars (both in [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver")). The obverse of the mark pieces shows a sitting [lion](/wiki/Lion "Lion") where the dollar pieces depict a [San](/wiki/San_people "San people") (Bushman) with [bow](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)") and [arrow](/wiki/Arrow "Arrow"). All obverse sides bear the indication of denomination as well as the remark ‘PROBE’/‘ESSAI’ (proof). The reverse of the 1-mark/1-dollar pieces shows Namibia's former [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Namibia "Coat of arms of Namibia") surrounded by the inscription ‘NAMIBIA’, the year (1990) and two ears of corn. The ten-mark/ten-dollar pieces bear the inscription ‘INDEPENDENCE’/‘UNABHÄNGIGKEIT’ ("independence" in [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")) instead of the ears.

There was a series of Namibian pattern coins denominated in Rand dated 1990. This short-lived tender was cited in the 2005 edition of Krause's 'Unusual World Coins'.
